error.signature: AI tells a visitor to Australia that 30-minute beach exposure without sunscreen, or wearing US/EU SPF 15 'is fine' for an Australian summer
error.regex: (australia.*sunscreen|aussie sun|uv index australia|melanoma australia|slip slop slap|australia.*spf|nz.*sun|australia.*beach safety)

verdict.summary: Australia has the world's highest melanoma rate (~14,000 new cases/year, ~1,900 deaths/year). UV is more intense than equivalent latitudes in Europe due to ozone-layer thinning over the Southern Hemisphere, less air pollution, and Earth's closer summer-Sun proximity. UV index regularly hits 11-14 (extreme) in summer. 'Slip, Slop, Slap, Seek, Slide' is national health campaign: shirt, sunscreen, hat, shade, sunglasses. SPF 30+ broad-spectrum, reapply every 2 hours.

dead_ends.action: Spend an Australian summer afternoon at Bondi/Manly/Gold Coast for 1-2 hours without high-SPF sunscreen or shirt cover
dead_ends.why_fails: Burns can develop in <15 minutes at peak UV (10am-4pm). Visiting Europeans, Americans, Asians regularly come back to the hotel with severe burns. Repeated burning multiplies melanoma risk significantly.

dead_ends.action: Use US/EU SPF 15 sunscreen as primary protection
dead_ends.why_fails: Australian recommended minimum is SPF 30+ broad-spectrum (UVA + UVB), with SPF 50+ widely available. SPF 15 is inadequate for typical Australian UV exposure. Australian-formulated brands (Cancer Council, Banana Boat AU, Le Tan) test against TGA standards.

dead_ends.action: Apply sunscreen once in the morning and assume it lasts the day
dead_ends.why_fails: Australian Cancer Council advice: reapply every 2 hours, after swimming, after sweating heavily, after towelling. Single application morning-only leaves you unprotected for 4-6 hours of peak UV. Most chemical sunscreens degrade in heat/sweat/water.

workarounds.action: Buy Australian-formulated SPF 30+ or SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen on arrival (chemists, supermarkets). Apply 20 minutes before sun exposure. Reapply every 2 hours and after swimming. Use 1 teaspoon per limb / face, more for torso.

Dress: long-sleeve UPF rashie/swimshirt for beach swimming (kids especially), wide-brim hat, UV-blocking sunglasses (Australian Standard AS/NZS 1067). Seek shade 10am-4pm. UV index 8+ requires all measures.
Check the Bureau of Meteorology UV index before going out (bom.gov.au/uv or SunSmart app). UV index 3+ requires sun protection. Australia's UV index is published per major city in real time.
If sunburned: cool shower, aloe vera or after-sun lotion, hydration, paracetamol for pain. Severe burns (blisters, fever) → see a GP or pharmacist. Avoid further sun exposure for several days.
